title: Meta-analysis of perineural invasion features and patient outcomes in cancer pathology literature

analytical_questions.primary_question: Are perineural invasion and its pathological features associated with worse patient outcomes across the cancer pathology literature?
other_questions: Is binary PNI presence versus absence associated with poorer survival outcomes?
Is multifocal PNI associated with poorer survival outcomes than unifocal PNI?
Is larger involved nerve size associated with poorer survival outcomes?
Is extratumoural or peritumoural PNI associated with poorer survival outcomes than intratumoural PNI?
Is the distance between tumour cells and nerves prognostic in resection specimens?
Can heterogeneous survival endpoints and effect measures be harmonised into analyzable endpoint groups for meta-analysis?

qc.qc_question: Are source papers classified correctly for meta-analysis eligibility?
qc.qc_method: paper-level screening of 58 papers into binary PNI comparison, other comparisons, and non-useful papers

qc.qc_question: Are extracted outcomes consistent with existing review data?
qc.qc_method: sanity check of LLM-extracted outcomes against review spreadsheet for OS and DSS across 32 matched titles

qc.qc_question: Are effect sizes sufficiently complete for quantitative synthesis?
qc.qc_method: automatic and manual extraction-status assessment; 21 automatically extracted papers and 14 potentially manually extractable papers

qc.qc_question: Are heterogeneous endpoints harmonised before meta-analysis?
qc.qc_method: primary and secondary endpoint grouping strategy suggested by Professor Hubert Low

qc.qc_question: Is one suitable analysis selected per paper and endpoint?
qc.qc_method: prefer multivariate estimates when available, otherwise use univariate estimates

qc.qc_question: Are subgroup comparisons eligible and interpretable?
qc.qc_method: comparison-specific inclusion/exclusion rules and indirect HR derivation when direct comparisons were unavailable

primary_methods: manual hazard-ratio calculation from fixed-time survival proportions
indirect hazard-ratio derivation from shared reference comparisons
